one_liner: A 22-year-old woman presents with 2 weeks of progressive psychiatric symptoms (paranoia, hallucinations), followed by seizures, orofacial dyskinesias, and autonomic instability, found to have CSF this condition antibodies and a pelvic ultrasound revealing an ovarian teratoma.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient's family reports that 3 weeks ago she developed a low-grade fever and headache for a few days (viral prodrome). Two weeks ago, she became increasingly anxious, irritable, and paranoid, believing her coworkers were trying to poison her. She was seen at a psychiatric urgent care and started on risperidone for 'new-onset psychosis.' Despite this, over the following days she developed visual hallucinations, insomnia, pressured speech, and disorganized behavior. Five days ago, she had a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. Since then, she has had progressive confusion, decreased responsiveness, and repetitive involuntary movements of her mouth, lips, and tongue (orofacial dyskinesias), along with rhythmic movements of her arms. She was brought to the ED today after becoming unresponsive. She has no psychiatric history. No recent drug use. No travel. She was in her usual state of health 3 weeks ago — a healthy graduate student.
stage2.medications: Risperidone 2 mg BID (started 10 days ago by psychiatry — now discontinued). Levetiracetam 500 mg BID (started 5 days ago after seizure).
stage2.family_history: No psychiatric or neurological diseases. No autoimmune diseases.
stage2.social_history: Graduate student in biology. Non-smoker. Social alcohol (stopped 3 weeks ago when ill). No illicit drug use (confirmed by roommate). Single. Sexually active with one male partner. No recent travel.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Young woman lying in bed, intermittently agitated with periods of unresponsiveness**; **Diaphoretic**. HEENT: **Pupils 5 mm bilaterally, reactive but sluggish**; **Cranial nerves: Difficult to assess formally**; **Continuous orofacial dyskinesias — repetitive chewing, lip smacking, tongue protrusion**; **Intermittent forceful jaw clenching**. Neuro: **GCS 8 (E2 V2 M4)**; **Reduced responsiveness, does not follow commands**; **Occasional purposeful withdrawal to pain**; **Involuntary movements: Orofacial dyskinesias (continuous)**; **Choreoathetoid movements of bilateral upper extremities**; No tremor; **Motor: Increased tone in all extremities (fluctuating — periods of rigidity alternating with hypotonia)**; **Intermittent catatonic posturing**; **DTRs: 3+ throughout**; **Babinski equivocal bilaterally**; **Autonomic instability: Heart rate fluctuates between 55-120 bpm**; **Blood pressure labile (168/98 to 88/52 over observation period)**; **Diaphoresis**; **Central hypoventilation intermittently noted**.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Mild leukocytosis; may be stress response or related to underlying teratoma
Mild hyponatremia, possibly from SIADH (can occur in encephalitis)
Normal; hypoglycemia excluded as cause of altered mental status

lab_results.result: WBC 48/uL (88% lymphocytes), protein 52 mg/dL, glucose 58 mg/dL
lab_results.clinical_significance: Lymphocytic pleocytosis with mild protein elevation consistent with autoimmune or viral encephalitis. Present in ~80% of anti-NMDAR encephalitis cases.
DIAGNOSTIC: this condition (GluN1 subunit) antibodies in CSF confirm the diagnosis. CSF testing is more sensitive than serum (serum can be false-negative in 13% of cases).
Positive serum antibody supports diagnosis but CSF is required for confirmation
Intrathecal IgG synthesis consistent with CNS autoimmune process
Mildly elevated from prolonged dyskinesias and rigidity; monitor for rhabdomyolysis

imaging_results.result: Subtle T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in bilateral medial temporal lobes. No mass, hemorrhage, or enhancement. Normal ventricles.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: MRI is normal or shows only subtle abnormalities in ~50% of anti-NMDAR encephalitis cases. The medial temporal FLAIR changes are nonspecific but consistent. A normal MRI does NOT exclude the diagnosis.

imaging_results.result: 3.5 cm complex mass in the right ovary containing echogenic foci with posterior shadowing (fat and calcification), consistent with a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst).
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Ovarian teratoma is found in ~40-50% of female anti-NMDAR encephalitis patients. Teratomas contain nervous tissue expressing NMDA receptors, which triggers the autoimmune response. Tumor removal is essential for treatment.

imaging_results.result: Diffuse generalized slowing (delta-theta range) with extreme delta brush pattern (rhythmic delta activity with superimposed fast beta activity).
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Extreme delta brush is a unique EEG pattern found in ~30% of anti-NMDAR encephalitis cases and is considered relatively specific for this condition. Diffuse slowing reflects encephalopathy.

key_tests: CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibodies (GluN1 subunit) — both serum and CSF
CSF HSV PCR (to exclude HSV encephalitis)
acceptable_tests: Comprehensive autoimmune encephalitis antibody panel (LGI1, CASPR2, AMPA, GABA-B)
Serum and CSF paraneoplastic antibody panel
stage3.low_yield_tests_note: A normal MRI does NOT exclude anti-NMDAR encephalitis — MRI is normal in ~50% of cases. CSF antibody testing is the gold standard.

stage5.final_clue: Pelvic ultrasound reveals a right ovarian teratoma.

debrief.classic_presentation: Young woman (teens-20s) with a viral-like prodrome followed by a stereotyped progression: (1) psychiatric symptoms (psychosis, paranoia, agitation — often initially misdiagnosed as primary psychiatric illness), (2) seizures, (3) movement disorder (orofacial dyskinesias, choreoathetosis, catatonia), (4) decreased consciousness, and (5) autonomic instability with central hypoventilation. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis and positive anti-NMDA receptor antibodies. Ovarian teratoma found in ~40-50% of females.
debrief.attending_mode_notes: This case challenges the clinician to recognize a neurological (autoimmune) cause of 'new-onset psychosis' in a young woman. The critical attending-level insight is that NEW psychiatric symptoms in a previously healthy young person, especially when accompanied by seizures, movement disorder, or autonomic instability, should trigger evaluation for autoimmune encephalitis BEFORE accepting a primary psychiatric diagnosis. The stereotyped progression (psychiatric > seizures > dyskinesias > catatonia > autonomic instability) is characteristic. Key pitfalls: (1) Attributing the presentation to a primary psychiatric disorder and treating with antipsychotics alone (risperidone in this case), which delays diagnosis, (2) Considering neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) given rigidity + autonomic instability + antipsychotic use — but NMS shows markedly elevated CK (usually >1000) and elevated prolactin, (3) Failing to check CSF antibodies (serum alone is insufficient — 13% are serum-negative/CSF-positive), (4) Failing to screen for ovarian teratoma — tumor removal is essential for full recovery and relapse prevention.
debrief.pathophysiology: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis is caused by IgG antibodies against the GluN1 (NR1) subunit of the NMDA receptor. These antibodies cause receptor internalization and cross-linking, reducing the density of surface NMDA receptors (not complement-mediated destruction — hence reversible with treatment). Reduced NMDA receptor function on GABAergic interneurons leads to DISINHIBITION of excitatory glutamatergic pathways (similar mechanism to the psychotomimetic effects of NMDA antagonists like ketamine and PCP). This frontal-subcortical disinhibition explains the psychiatric symptoms, while involvement of hippocampal, basal ganglia, brainstem, and autonomic circuits explains the seizures, movement disorder, catatonia, decreased consciousness, and autonomic instability. In teratoma-associated cases, neural tissue within the teratoma expresses NMDA receptors, triggering an immune response that cross-reacts with brain NMDA receptors.
debrief.treatment_overview: Treatment follows a 3-pronged approach: 1. TUMOR REMOVAL: If ovarian teratoma is present, urgent surgical resection is critical — patients with teratoma removal have better outcomes and lower relapse rates. 2. FIRST-LINE IMMUNOTHERAPY: IV methylprednisolone (1 g/day x 5 days) + IVIg (0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days) OR plasma exchange. Start immediately upon clinical suspicion (do not wait for antibody results). 3. SECOND-LINE IMMUNOTHERAPY (if no improvement after 2 weeks): Rituximab (anti-CD20) and/or cyclophosphamide. Supportive ICU care: seizure management (benzodiazepines, levetiracetam — avoid carbamazepine/phenytoin), autonomic monitoring, ventilatory support for central hypoventilation, sedation for severe dyskinesias (dexmedetomidine, midazolam). Avoid antipsychotics if possible (can worsen dyskinesias and autonomic instability). Outcomes: 75-80% achieve good recovery (mRS 0-2) but recovery is slow (months to years). Relapses occur in 12-20% — monitor antibody titers and screen for new teratomas.
high_yield_pearls: New-onset psychiatric symptoms in a young woman that rapidly progress to include seizures, movement disorder, or autonomic instability should prompt evaluation for anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis — do NOT accept a primary psychiatric diagnosis without ruling out autoimmune encephalitis.
CSF anti-NMDA receptor antibody testing is REQUIRED — serum testing alone is insufficient because 13% of cases are serum-negative but CSF-positive.
Extreme delta brush on EEG (rhythmic delta with superimposed beta activity) is a unique pattern that is relatively specific for anti-NMDAR encephalitis (~30% of cases).

Ovarian teratoma screening is mandatory in ALL female patients with anti-NMDAR encephalitis — tumor removal dramatically improves outcomes and reduces relapse. Repeat imaging if initial screening is negative.

The mechanism of anti-NMDAR encephalitis (antibody-mediated receptor internalization) is REVERSIBLE — unlike complement-mediated neuronal destruction — explaining the potential for excellent recovery with early aggressive immunotherapy.
Post-HSV encephalitis anti-NMDAR encephalitis: ~20% of patients recovering from HSV encephalitis develop anti-NMDA receptor antibodies weeks later — HSV PCR should always be checked in the workup.
sources: Dalmau J, et al. Anti-NMDA-receptor encephalitis: case series and analysis of the effects of antibodies. Lancet Neurol. 2008 — https://www.thelancet.com/
Graus F, et al. A clinical approach to diagnosis of autoimmune encephalitis. Lancet Neurol. 2016 — https://www.thelancet.com/
